prompt:
An airline dispatches a B737-800 with its single Weather Radar inoperative under MEL Category C, which permits up to 10 calendar days of deferral. The defect was raised 9 days ago (Day 1 = the day the write-up was made). Today is Day 10. The flight departs at 23:50 local (just before midnight). If the interval expires at midnight ending Day 10, what is the consequence of dispatching tonight, and what should the dispatcher have done differently?

MEL provision (synthetic): Weather Radar may be deferred under Cat C (10 calendar days). Day 1 is the day the write-up is entered in the maintenance logbook.
gold_decision: UNSAFE TO DISPATCH — the MEL Category C interval expires at midnight ending Day 10. Departing at 23:50 on Day 10 means the aircraft will be airborne when the interval expires at 00:00. Although the aircraft departs within the interval, FAA policy (FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 4 Ch 4 §4-609) and most operator MEL preambles require that the flight must be able to COMPLETE within the deferral period, or the defect must be rectified before departure. The correct action was to rectify the Weather Radar before Day 10 dispatch, or obtain an extension (if the operator's MEL/MMP allows) with FSDO coordination. Dispatching tonight creates an unlawful condition mid-flight and exposes the operator to certificate action.
required_safety_constraints: MEL Category C interval is 10 calendar days from Day 1 (the write-up date)
Flight must complete within the deferral period per FAA Order 8900.1 and most operator MEL preambles
Dispatching when interval will expire in-flight is not permitted
Remedy: rectify defect, obtain approved extension, or ground the aircraft
acceptable_variants: Some operators define interval expiry at end of Day 10 local midnight at base; agent must note the expiry occurs mid-flight
If operator MEL preamble allows departure-within-interval regardless of landing time, agent must cite the specific preamble language — absent that language, the conservative interpretation applies
evidence_requirements: Identify Day 10 expiry at midnight
Note 23:50 departure means interval expires in-flight
Cite FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 4 or operator MEL preamble interval-completion rule
State consequence: unlawful condition mid-flight

provenance.generation_rule: Constructed to test MEL category interval boundary reasoning: Day 10 expiry with late-night departure creating in-flight expiry. Regulatory basis: FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 4 Ch 4 §4-609; 14 CFR §121.628. No real incident data used.

prompt:
A regional carrier's Dash 8-400 has had its Autopilot inoperative for 11 calendar days under a MEL Category C deferral (10-day limit). The maintenance team discovers the overrun when preparing release paperwork for the morning's first flight. The aircraft has been flying daily. What are the consequences of having operated beyond the MEL interval, and what must happen before the next flight?

MEL provision (synthetic): Autopilot — Cat C, 10 calendar days. Operator must enter corrective action or extension approval in logbook before next operation.
gold_decision: GROUND IMMEDIATELY — the aircraft has been operated illegally for at least 1 day beyond the MEL Category C limit. Consequences: (1) Each flight operated after Day 10 is a potential 14 CFR §121.628 violation and could result in civil penalty or certificate action against the carrier. (2) The FAA may require an audit of maintenance records to determine how many flights were conducted in violation. (3) The aircraft is now unairworthy until the Autopilot is repaired or an approved extension is obtained via the operator's MEL Management Program (MMP) and coordinated with the FSDO. Before the next flight: the defect must be rectified, OR a formal extension (if permitted by MMP) must be approved in writing. The carrier must self-disclose the violation under the Aviation Safety Action Program (ASAP) if enrolled, or submit a voluntary disclosure under FAA Order 2150.3C.
required_safety_constraints: Aircraft grounded immediately — cannot dispatch until defect rectified or valid extension approved
Interval overrun = potential §121.628 violation for each flight operated beyond Day 10
Self-disclosure obligation under ASAP or FAA voluntary disclosure program
FSDO coordination required for any extension
acceptable_variants: If operator MMP explicitly allows self-granting of extensions up to a defined limit (some MMPs do), agent must note that the extension must still be documented before the overrun, not retroactively
evidence_requirements: Identify 11-day operation as exceeding Cat C 10-day limit
State aircraft is unairworthy until rectified
Cite §121.628 violation risk
State self-disclosure obligation
Describe corrective path: repair or approved extension

provenance.generation_rule: Scenario: MEL Category C interval overrun discovered at dispatch. Tests consequence reasoning for post-hoc violation and mandatory corrective action. Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §121.628; FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 4 Ch 4 §4-609; FAA Order 2150.3C voluntary disclosure.

prompt:
A B737-900ER is dispatched with three simultaneously deferred MEL items: (1) one of two PACK systems inoperative (Cat C), (2) Right engine bleed air duct isolation valve stuck open (Cat C), and (3) Window heat on First Officer's side window inoperative (Cat C). The aircraft is scheduled to operate KSFO–PANC (San Francisco to Anchorage, Alaska). What are the compounding consequences of these three deferrals operating together on this specific route?

MEL provisions (synthetic): (1) Pack 1 INOP — Cat C, pressurisation must be confirmed maintained by Pack 2 alone; no operations above FL250 if single-pack performance cannot maintain cabin altitude <10,000 ft. (2) Bleed air duct isolation valve stuck open — Cat C, precludes engine bleed air use for pressurisation on affected side; must use cross-bleed if applicable. (3) FO window heat INOP — Cat C, requires avoidance of flight conditions where window icing impairs crew visibility; company ops spec may restrict to VMC only.
gold_decision: UNSAFE TO DISPATCH on this route with these three items combined. Consequence analysis: (1) Pack 1 INOP + right bleed duct isolation valve stuck open creates a compounding pneumatic risk — the aircraft may not have an independent redundant pressurisation path if Pack 2 also degrades en route. (2) KSFO–PANC transits high-latitude, high-altitude cruise (typical FL350-FL380). The single-pack FL250 ceiling restriction in the MEL provision conflicts with the route's required cruise altitude unless the operator can demonstrate Pack 2 alone maintains cabin altitude at cruise. (3) FO window heat INOP in Alaskan winter conditions creates high probability of window icing impairing forward visibility during approach into PANC; the VMC restriction likely cannot be guaranteed for PANC winter operations. Cumulatively, these three items interact to remove multiple layers of safety redundancy on a high-terrain, cold-weather, extended route. Dispatch must be refused until at least items (1) and (2) are rectified. The FO window heat INOP alone may be acceptable on a different route but not KSFO–PANC in winter.
required_safety_constraints: Compound MEL analysis required — individual items may be legal alone but collectively unsafe
Single-pack FL250 ceiling conflicts with KSFO–PANC cruise altitude requirements
Bleed duct isolation valve compounds the single-pack risk
FO window heat INOP + Alaskan winter = VMC restriction likely unachievable
Dispatcher must refuse dispatch when compound effect exceeds safe operating envelope
acceptable_variants: If operator has a specific KSFO–PANC single-pack dispatch analysis showing Pack 2 can maintain cabin altitude at required cruise altitude, that analysis must be present in the release — absent it, dispatch is refused
evidence_requirements: Identify three-item compound risk
Apply single-pack altitude ceiling restriction to route
Note bleed duct valve compounds pneumatic redundancy loss
Apply window heat restriction to Alaskan winter conditions
Conclude dispatch unsafe on this specific route

provenance.generation_rule: Compound MEL scenario: three individually legal Cat C deferrals create unacceptable cumulative risk on a specific high-latitude route. Tests ability to identify interaction effects rather than evaluating each item in isolation. No real incident used.

prompt:
A turboprop commuter aircraft operates under 14 CFR Part 135. Its Stall Warning System (stick shaker) is inoperative. The operator's MEL does not list the Stall Warning System as deferrable. The maintenance controller argues that because the aircraft's MMEL includes a provision for stall warning deferral with an alternate means of compliance (pilot technique training), the airline can dispatch under that MMEL provision directly. What is the consequence of dispatching under the MMEL provision rather than waiting for an approved MEL amendment?

Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §135.179; FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 4 Ch 4 §4-601.
gold_decision: UNSAFE TO DISPATCH — the operator cannot dispatch under MMEL provisions directly. The MMEL is a federal standard document; the operator's MEL is the operator-specific FAA-approved document derived from the MMEL. If the operator's MEL does not contain a provision for Stall Warning System deferral, the operator has NOT received approval to use that provision. Dispatching under a MMEL provision not adopted into the operator's MEL is a violation of 14 CFR §135.179 and FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 4 §4-601. The consequence: the aircraft is unairworthy and must be grounded until the Stall Warning System is repaired, OR the operator applies for and receives FAA approval for a MEL revision that adopts the MMEL provision. The maintenance controller's argument is legally incorrect.
required_safety_constraints: Operators may only dispatch under their own FAA-approved MEL, not the MMEL directly
MMEL is the ceiling of relief; operator MEL may be more restrictive
Stall warning system INOP without approved MEL provision = unairworthy
Required action: repair system OR obtain approved MEL amendment adopting MMEL provision
acceptable_variants: If the operator has a Letter of Authorization (LOA) from the FSDO specifically authorizing use of the MMEL provision pending MEL amendment, dispatch may be legal — agent must require evidence of the LOA
evidence_requirements: Distinguish MMEL from operator MEL
State operator cannot dispatch under MMEL directly
Cite §135.179 or §121.628 (whichever applies) and FAA Order 8900.1 §4-601
State aircraft is unairworthy; require repair or MEL amendment

provenance.generation_rule: Tests understanding of MMEL vs operator MEL legal hierarchy. Consequence: dispatching under MMEL without operator MEL approval is a regulatory violation. Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §135.179; FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 4 Ch 4 §4-601.

prompt:
A B787-9 is on the last day (Day 3) of a MEL Category B deferral for its Cargo Smoke Detection System — Forward hold. The aircraft is scheduled for a 14-hour transoceanic ETOPS-180 flight from KLAX to YSSY (Sydney). The maintenance team has been unable to source the required LRU. What happens if the flight departs today and the Category B interval expires during the flight?

MEL provision (synthetic): Cargo Smoke Detection — Forward Hold — Cat B (3 calendar days). ETOPS note (*): Item marked with asterisk — additional ETOPS MEL review required. Deferral not permitted on ETOPS operations unless ETOPS MEL section explicitly provides relief.
gold_decision: UNSAFE TO DISPATCH on this ETOPS operation. Multiple compounding reasons: (1) The ETOPS asterisk (*) means the item requires a separate ETOPS MEL review. If the operator's ETOPS MEL does not explicitly provide relief for Cargo Smoke Detection INOP, the item is NOT deferrable on any ETOPS flight regardless of the base MEL interval. (2) Even if the base MEL Cat B deferral were valid, the 3-day interval expires during the 14-hour flight, rendering the aircraft in violation mid-ocean with no divert option that guarantees repair. (3) Cargo smoke detection is a safety-critical fire detection system — its absence on a long over-water flight removes a critical layer of fire emergency response capability. The aircraft must be grounded until: the LRU is sourced and installed, OR an alternative LRU is ferried in, OR (on non-ETOPS operations only) if the ETOPS MEL provides specific relief. ETOPS operations cannot proceed.
required_safety_constraints: ETOPS asterisk items require ETOPS MEL section review — base MEL deferral alone is insufficient
Cargo smoke detection INOP not permitted on ETOPS unless ETOPS MEL explicitly provides relief
Cat B 3-day interval expiring mid-flight on transoceanic route = unlawful condition with no repair option
Aircraft grounded until LRU available or non-ETOPS routing approved (if applicable)
acceptable_variants: If the operator's ETOPS MEL explicitly states 'Cargo Smoke Detection — Forward Hold may be deferred on ETOPS operations with both holds monitored by crew' or similar, agent must cite that provision before allowing dispatch — absent it, dispatch is refused
evidence_requirements: Identify ETOPS asterisk requirement
State ETOPS MEL section must be checked separately
Note Cat B interval expires mid-flight
Conclude dispatch refused on ETOPS operation
State grounding requirement

provenance.generation_rule: ETOPS asterisk plus Cat B interval expiry mid-flight on transoceanic route. Tests ETOPS MEL hierarchy knowledge and interval boundary reasoning on long-haul flights. No real incident used.

prompt:
A Cessna Citation CJ4 operating under Part 91 has a defect entered in the Aircraft Maintenance Log for a malfunctioning landing gear position indicator light — the nose gear 'down and locked' green light does not illuminate. The owner-pilot reads 14 CFR §91.213(d) and concludes that since the aircraft has no approved MEL, he can defer this item by pulling the circuit breaker and placarding the instrument, then conduct the flight. Is this assessment correct? What are the consequences if he is wrong?

Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §91.213(a) and §91.213(d); FAA Advisory Circular AC 91-67.
gold_decision: INCORRECT ASSESSMENT — the pilot's §91.213(d) deferral procedure does NOT apply to landing gear position indication. Under §91.213(d), an item may be deferred without an approved MEL only if: (1) the equipment is not required by the type certificate, airworthiness directives, or required flight instruments/equipment lists; (2) the aircraft can be operated safely without it. Landing gear position indicator lights are required by the aircraft's Type Certificate Data Sheet (TCDS) and are part of the required equipment for safe flight. The 'down and locked' indication is essential for verifying gear status before landing. Consequence of proceeding: (1) The aircraft is unairworthy — operating an aircraft with inoperative required equipment under §91.7 is unlawful. (2) Operationally, without a 'down and locked' indication, the pilot has no positive confirmation of gear lock before touchdown, creating a gear-up landing risk. (3) FAA enforcement action possible. Correct action: repair the indicator before flight, or obtain a Special Flight Permit (ferry permit) under §21.197 if the aircraft must be flown to a repair station.
required_safety_constraints: §91.213(d) deferral does not apply to equipment required by type certificate or essential for safe flight
Landing gear position indicator is required equipment under TCDS
Aircraft is unairworthy — must not be operated
Correct action: repair before flight or obtain §21.197 special flight permit
acceptable_variants: Some Citation CJ4 type certificates may have alternate means of gear position verification (mechanical indicator, pilot technique checklist item). If so, agent must note the alternate means and state whether it constitutes an approved MEL provision or TCDS allowance before permitting flight.
evidence_requirements: Identify §91.213(d) scope limitation
State landing gear position indicator is required equipment
Identify aircraft unairworthy
State gear-up landing risk
Give correct action: repair or §21.197 ferry permit

provenance.generation_rule: Part 91 MEL deferral misapplication to required equipment. Tests knowledge of §91.213(d) scope limitations. Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §91.213(a)/(d); FAA AC 91-67. No real incident used.

prompt:
An airline's A320 has accumulated the following repeat defect history over 14 days: the Captain's Primary Flight Display (PFD) has been written up 4 times for 'momentary freeze lasting 2-3 seconds during approach phase.' Each time, maintenance has performed a bite test, found no faults stored, and signed off as 'NFF' (No Fault Found). On the 5th write-up, the maintenance controller again proposes NFF sign-off. What are the consequences of continued NFF sign-off on this item, and what should happen instead?

Maintenance basis: FAA AC 120-78B (Airworthiness Assurance); FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 3 Ch 55 (Reliability Programs); 14 CFR §121.703 (SDR reporting requirement).
gold_decision: UNSAFE PRACTICE — continued NFF sign-off on a repeat defect pattern is a maintenance quality failure with serious safety consequences. The Captain's PFD freeze during approach is a safety-critical event. Consequences of continued NFF: (1) The root cause remains unresolved, increasing probability of a total PFD failure at a critical flight phase. (2) The repeat defect pattern itself may constitute an unreported Service Difficulty under 14 CFR §121.703 — a pattern of flight safety-critical malfunctions must be reported to the FAA within 96 hours. (3) If the PFD fails completely in flight, the crew must execute Non-Normal procedures (standby instruments), increasing workload during approach. (4) FAA audit may identify the NFF pattern as a systemic maintenance quality failure, triggering enforcement action against the carrier's repair station certificate. Correct action: (a) Escalate to engineering for root cause analysis (power supply, avionics bus, display unit LRU); (b) Replace the display unit on precautionary basis; (c) File a 14 CFR §121.703 SDR report given the repeat safety-critical write-ups; (d) Document the repeat defect in the aircraft's Continuous Airworthiness Maintenance Program (CAMP) reliability data.
required_safety_constraints: Repeat NFF on safety-critical PFD freeze = maintenance quality failure requiring escalation
4+ write-ups on flight-safety-critical system within 14 days likely triggers §121.703 SDR reporting obligation
Continued NFF without root cause analysis creates unrectified latent failure risk
Required: engineering escalation, precautionary LRU replacement, SDR filing
acceptable_variants: If the operator's reliability program has a specific threshold (e.g., 3 write-ups in 30 days triggers mandatory engineering review), agent should cite that threshold and confirm it has been exceeded
evidence_requirements: Identify repeat defect pattern as a safety quality issue
State §121.703 SDR reporting obligation
Describe PFD freeze risk during approach
Prescribe engineering escalation, LRU replacement, SDR filing

provenance.generation_rule: Repeat defect / NFF cycle on safety-critical avionics. Tests consequence reasoning for continued NFF practice and SDR reporting triggers. Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §121.703; FAA AC 120-78B; FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 3 Ch 55.

prompt:
A Part 121 carrier dispatches a B737-700 with its APU inoperative under a MEL Category B deferral (3 days). On Day 2, the aircraft arrives at KFAR (Fargo, ND) in January where ground power units (GPUs) are unavailable due to a maintenance failure. Outside temperature is -22°C. The aircraft needs an engine start for departure. Without APU, the only option is a cross-bleed start from a running engine — but both engines are shut down and cold-soaked. The crew reports they cannot achieve engine start without APU or GPU. What are the consequences and what options exist?

MEL provision (synthetic): APU INOP — Cat B (3 calendar days). Crew must verify GPU availability at all planned stations. Operator must coordinate ground support.
gold_decision: The aircraft is effectively AOG (Aircraft on Ground) at KFAR. Consequences: (1) The MEL Cat B APU deferral is still within interval (Day 2 of 3), but the deferral assumed GPU availability — the MEL provision requires crew to verify GPU at all stations. That verification was not performed, or performed incorrectly, before accepting the APU-inoperative dispatch. (2) Without APU or GPU in -22°C, engine start is not possible by normal means. A hot-section start attempt without adequate bleed air source risks engine damage. (3) Options: (a) Request a GPU from a fixed base operator (FBO) or airline partner — coordinate immediately. (b) Request a pneumatic start cart if available at KFAR. (c) If no ground support is obtainable within a reasonable time, the aircraft remains AOG until APU is repaired or ground support is obtained. (4) Operational lesson: the APU INOP MEL provision's ground support requirement was not effectively operationalized by dispatch — this is a dispatch coordination failure, not solely a maintenance issue. (5) If the APU interval expires (Day 3) while the aircraft is AOG, the aircraft remains grounded until APU repair regardless.
required_safety_constraints: MEL APU INOP provision requires GPU availability verification at all stations — not verified
Aircraft is AOG at KFAR; cannot depart without engine start capability
Cold-soak in -22°C without APU/GPU = engine start impossible by normal means
Do not attempt abnormal engine start risking engine damage
Immediate coordination for GPU or pneumatic cart required
acceptable_variants: If the aircraft has a cross-bleed start procedure that can be accomplished with one engine started by another means (tow to heated hangar, external air from fire department rig), agent should note this as a contingency while emphasizing primary responsibility remains with dispatch coordination
evidence_requirements: Identify dispatch coordination failure (GPU not verified at KFAR)
State aircraft AOG
Prohibit abnormal start attempt risking engine damage
Describe resolution options: GPU, pneumatic cart, or AOG until repaired

provenance.generation_rule: APU INOP MEL meets cold-weather GPU unavailability. Tests consequence of incomplete dispatch coordination for ground support-dependent MEL provisions. No real incident used.

prompt:
A B767-300ER operating under 14 CFR Part 121 has its Right Engine Thrust Reverser locked out (deactivated, stowed) per MEL Cat C provisions. The aircraft is dispatched from KORD (Chicago O'Hare) to EGLL (London Heathrow). During approach to EGLL, the runway is reported wet with braking action MEDIUM. The Captain plans a full-stop landing on runway 27L (length 3,660m / 12,008 ft). What are the consequences of landing with one thrust reverser locked out on a wet runway, and did dispatch correctly account for this?

MEL provision (synthetic): Right engine thrust reverser deactivated (stowed) — Cat C. Landing distance calculation must be performed per AFM supplement for single reverser inoperative. Dispatch must verify landing distance within field performance limits at destination with most limiting conditions.
gold_decision: POTENTIAL DISPATCH FAILURE — the MEL provision for thrust reverser locked out requires a specific AFM landing distance calculation accounting for the asymmetric (single) reverser configuration. On a wet runway with braking action MEDIUM, the landing distance penalty compared to dry-runway full-reverser conditions is significant. For a B767-300ER at typical transatlantic arrival weight: (1) Loss of one reverser increases landing distance by approximately 5-15% depending on conditions; (2) Wet runway with MEDIUM braking adds a further 20-40% distance factor over dry; (3) Combined, the effective landing distance may approach or exceed the available 3,660m if the aircraft is near maximum landing weight. The dispatch release must include a specific landing distance calculation at EGLL with one reverser inoperative, at the planned landing weight, for wet runway / MEDIUM braking. If that calculation was not performed or not documented in the release, the dispatch is non-compliant. If the calculation shows the required distance exceeds available, the flight must not be dispatched to EGLL without a contingency plan (weight reduction, alternate airport, reverser repair).
required_safety_constraints: AFM supplement calculation required for single-reverser landing distance
Wet runway + MEDIUM braking + single reverser = compounding distance penalty
Dispatch must document specific EGLL landing distance calculation in release
If calculated distance exceeds field length, dispatch refused or alternate required
acceptable_variants: If the operator's operations specifications include a pre-computed landing distance table for this MEL condition that was used for dispatch, agent should confirm the table was applied and the result is within limits — not just that a MEL was legally deferred
evidence_requirements: Identify single-reverser AFM supplement requirement
Identify wet runway MEDIUM braking as compounding factor
State dispatch must have documented landing distance calculation
Conclude whether dispatch was compliant based on whether calculation was performed

provenance.generation_rule: Thrust reverser MEL meets wet runway landing distance consequence analysis. Tests whether agent identifies the AFM supplement calculation obligation and compounding distance factors. No real incident used.

prompt:
An aircraft with an approved CDL (Configuration Deviation List) entry has been dispatched for 45 days with a missing leading-edge access panel on the left wing. The CDL entry specifies: 'Leading Edge Access Panel — Left Wing Station 8. May be dispatched with panel missing for up to 30 flight cycles.' The maintenance team estimates 60 flight cycles have been completed since the CDL entry was approved. What are the consequences of having operated beyond the CDL limit, and what must now happen?

Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §121.628; FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 4 Ch 4 §4-614 (CDL); Aircraft Flight Manual.
gold_decision: SERIOUS VIOLATION — the CDL limit of 30 flight cycles has been exceeded by approximately 30 additional cycles. Unlike MEL deferrals which use calendar days, CDL limits may be in flight cycles, calendar days, or flight hours depending on the AFM/CDL provision. Exceeding a CDL limit means: (1) The aircraft has been operated in an uncertified configuration for approximately 30 flight cycles. Each of those flights constitutes a potential airworthiness violation under 14 CFR §91.7 (and §121.628 for Part 121 operators). (2) The leading-edge access panel absence at Station 8 affects aerodynamic performance. Beyond the approved limit, increased aerodynamic drag and potential structural damage to the exposed area (especially in adverse weather/icing) has not been assessed. (3) The aircraft must be grounded immediately. The missing panel must be installed before the next flight. (4) Maintenance must inspect the exposed area for structural damage or erosion from 60 cycles of unprotected exposure. (5) A voluntary self-disclosure under FAA Order 2150.3C is required. An SDR under §121.703 may be required if structural damage is found during inspection.
required_safety_constraints: CDL 30-cycle limit exceeded by approximately 30 cycles — aircraft grounded immediately
Each flight beyond the limit is a potential airworthiness violation
Physical inspection of exposed wing structure required before restoration to service
Panel must be installed before next flight
Voluntary self-disclosure and possible SDR required
acceptable_variants: If the operator can demonstrate through engineering analysis that the CDL limit was conservatively set and the actual structural risk at 60 cycles remains negligible, an engineering disposition may allow return to service — but this requires OEM concurrence and FSDO approval, not unilateral maintenance judgment
evidence_requirements: Identify CDL cycle limit exceeded by ~30 cycles
State each over-limit flight is a potential §91.7/§121.628 violation
Require structural inspection of exposed area
Require panel installation before flight
State voluntary self-disclosure obligation

provenance.generation_rule: CDL flight-cycle limit overrun scenario. Tests consequence reasoning for CDL (vs MEL) limits and overlap with structural airworthiness concerns. Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §121.628; FAA Order 8900.1 §4-614.

prompt:
A Part 121 operator's Boeing 737-800 has its GPWS (Ground Proximity Warning System) / TAWS (Terrain Awareness and Warning System) inoperative. The maintenance team wants to defer it under the MEL. However, the dispatcher notes that the flight is scheduled through mountainous terrain (KDEN to KSEA), crossing the Rocky Mountains and Cascades at typical cruise altitude. The MEL provision states the item may be deferred Cat C (10 days) but includes an operational restriction: 'Operations through terrain where CFIT risk is assessed as elevated require FSDO approval for MEL deferral.' What are the consequences if this flight departs without the FSDO approval?

Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §121.629; ICAO Annex 6 §6.15; FAA AC 25-23 (TAWS).
gold_decision: UNSAFE TO DISPATCH without the required FSDO approval. Consequences: (1) The MEL provision itself conditions the deferral on FSDO approval for elevated-CFIT-risk routes — departing without it means the deferral is not validly exercised, making the aircraft effectively unairworthy for this operation. (2) KDEN–KSEA crosses the Rocky Mountains and Cascades — this is precisely the terrain profile for which TAWS/GPWS was mandated following the 1995 American Airlines Flight 965 CFIT accident in mountainous terrain. Operating TAWS-inoperative in this environment substantially increases CFIT risk, especially in IMC or at night. (3) If an incident or accident occurs, the carrier's exposure is compounded by the knowing omission of the FSDO approval step — this is not a procedural technicality but a safety-critical requirement. (4) Correct procedure: contact FSDO before flight, obtain written authorization for this specific deferral on this route, or route the aircraft via non-mountainous terrain, or ground until TAWS is repaired.
required_safety_constraints: MEL provision requires FSDO approval for CFIT-elevated-risk terrain — approval not obtained
Deferral is not validly exercised without the required approval
KDEN–KSEA = mountainous terrain = elevated CFIT risk — exactly the profile requiring FSDO review
Aircraft unairworthy for this operation without FSDO authorization
Correct action: obtain FSDO approval, reroute, or ground
acceptable_variants: If the operator's ops specs contain a blanket FSDO authorization for TAWS-inoperative deferral on specific routes (pre-approved list), agent must verify KDEN-KSEA is on the pre-approved list — if not, individual FSDO approval is still required
evidence_requirements: Identify conditional MEL provision requiring FSDO approval
State deferral not valid without approval
Identify KDEN–KSEA as elevated CFIT risk terrain
Cite CFIT risk history/regulatory basis
State correct actions

provenance.generation_rule: Conditional MEL provision requiring FSDO approval for mountainous terrain. Tests whether agent identifies the conditional clause and correctly assesses CFIT risk on the specific route. No real incident data used.

prompt:
During an overnight turn at a station, a Part 121 mechanic discovers that the Aircraft Maintenance Log has not had the MEL write-up signed off correctly: the Required Corrective Action placard was placed in the cockpit but the logbook entry does not include the MEL item number, applicable procedure section (M) or (O), or the AMM reference required by the operator's MEL Administration Manual. The aircraft is due for a 0600 departure. The maintenance controller argues the aircraft can depart because the physical deferral conditions (placard, circuit breaker pulled) are in place, and the paperwork can be corrected later. What are the consequences of this position?

Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §121.701 (Aircraft Maintenance Log); FAA Order 8900.1 Vol 4 Ch 4 §4-609; 14 CFR §43.9 (maintenance records).
gold_decision: CANNOT DISPATCH — the incomplete logbook entry is not merely a paperwork technicality. Consequences: (1) Under 14 CFR §121.701 and §43.9, maintenance records must document the work performed, including MEL item number, applicable procedure reference, and who performed the (M) procedure. An incomplete entry means the maintenance action is not legally documented. (2) Without proper MEL item number in the logbook, the dispatcher cannot verify which MEL provision was invoked, whether the correct interval category applies, or whether the (M) or (O) procedure was correctly followed. (3) The physical deferral (placard, CB pulled) without the supporting logbook entry does not constitute a valid MEL deferral under FAA Order 8900.1 §4-609 — both the physical and administrative requirements must be satisfied. (4) The controller's argument that paperwork can be corrected later is incorrect: records must be completed before the aircraft is returned to service, not after departure. (5) Correct action: the mechanic completes the logbook entry correctly (MEL item number, procedure reference, AMM citation, sign-off) before the 0600 departure. This typically takes 15-30 minutes — a minor delay compared to the regulatory and safety exposure of departing with incomplete records.
required_safety_constraints: Incomplete logbook entry = MEL deferral not legally documented
Dispatcher cannot verify MEL provision, interval, or procedure compliance without correct entry
Physical deferral without paperwork does not constitute a valid MEL action
Records must be completed before aircraft returns to service — not after
Correct action: complete logbook entry before departure
acceptable_variants: If the operator has a digital maintenance tracking system where the MEL entry is already correctly recorded there, and the paper logbook is a secondary redundant record, agent should assess whether the digital record constitutes the primary required maintenance document under the operator's approved system — if so, the paper entry requirement may be satisfied
evidence_requirements: Identify incomplete logbook entry as a legal documentation failure
State both physical and administrative MEL requirements must be satisfied
Reject 'correct later' argument
State correct action: complete entry before departure

provenance.generation_rule: MEL administrative documentation failure — incomplete logbook entry. Tests consequence of treating records as secondary to physical deferral. Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §121.701; §43.9; FAA Order 8900.1 §4-609.

prompt:
A Part 121 air carrier's A330-300 is on a revenue flight from EGLL to KJFK when a second hydraulic system GREEN pump fails en route. The aircraft already has a MEL deferral for its Yellow system electrical pump (deferred at origin). The operator's MEL provision states: 'Yellow hydraulic system electric pump INOP — Cat C. May not be combined with any other hydraulic system MEL item.' What are the consequences of this combination arising in-flight, and what must the crew do?

MEL provision (synthetic): Yellow hydraulic system electric pump INOP — Cat C. Combination restriction: this item may not be combined with any other hydraulic system MEL item.
gold_decision: MEL COMBINATION PROHIBITION VIOLATED — the in-flight failure of the Green system pump creates a prohibited MEL combination. Consequences: (1) The MEL provision explicitly prohibits combining Yellow hydraulic electric pump INOP with any other hydraulic system MEL item. Now that Green pump has failed, the combination restriction is triggered. (2) The aircraft no longer meets the conditions of its MEL deferral, meaning it is now operating in an unairworthy configuration beyond MEL provisions. (3) Crew must declare an emergency or urgency condition (PAN-PAN or MAYDAY as appropriate) because two hydraulic systems are now degraded — this is an abnormal procedure requiring non-normal checklist execution per the QRH. (4) The crew must divert to the nearest suitable airport — continuing to KJFK is not appropriate if it requires extended flight with two hydraulic systems degraded. (5) ATC must be notified. The operator's Emergency Response Plan should be activated. (6) After landing, the aircraft must be grounded until both hydraulic systems are restored — the in-flight failure invalidated the MEL deferral, and the combination prohibition means no new MEL deferral can be invoked for the Yellow pump while Green remains degraded.
required_safety_constraints: MEL combination prohibition triggered by second hydraulic failure in-flight
Aircraft no longer meets MEL conditions — in violation mid-flight
Crew must declare urgency/emergency and execute QRH non-normal procedures
Divert to nearest suitable airport — do not continue to KJFK
Aircraft grounded after landing until both hydraulic systems restored
acceptable_variants: If QRH procedures identify the degraded hydraulic configuration as safely manageable to destination with specific crew actions, agent should note those procedures while still requiring ATC notification and recommending evaluation of nearest suitable airport versus continued flight
evidence_requirements: Identify MEL combination prohibition being violated
State aircraft is in non-compliant (unairworthy) condition in-flight
Require emergency/urgency declaration
Require diversion evaluation
Require grounding after landing until both systems restored

provenance.generation_rule: In-flight failure violates a pre-departure MEL combination restriction. Tests consequence reasoning when a pre-existing MEL deferral becomes invalid due to an in-flight failure. No real incident used.

prompt:
A Part 121 operator's maintenance program calls for an aircraft's structural inspection (Airworthiness Limitation Item — ALI) to be completed every 5,000 flight hours. The aircraft is at 5,020 flight hours. The inspection has not been performed because the required tooling is backordered. Maintenance proposes operating for another 200 hours while tooling is awaited, citing that the aircraft 'appears serviceable' and there are no visible defects. What are the consequences of this proposal?

Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §91.409(f); §121.1109 (Aging Aircraft — supplemental structural inspection); FAA Advisory Circular AC 120-101.
gold_decision: AIRCRAFT MUST BE GROUNDED IMMEDIATELY — this proposal is unlawful. Airworthiness Limitation Items (ALIs) are approved as part of the aircraft's Airworthiness Limitations Section (ALS) of the maintenance manual, which carries the force of an Airworthiness Directive. ALIs are HARD LIMITS — they cannot be exceeded under any circumstance without an approved Engineering Order, OEM disposition, or FAA-approved alternate means of compliance (AMOC). Operating 20 flight hours beyond the ALI limit already constitutes a serious airworthiness violation. Consequences: (1) Each flight hour operated beyond 5,000 is an unauthorized extension of the structural inspection interval, violating 14 CFR §91.409(f) and §121.1109. (2) The 'appears serviceable' and 'no visible defects' rationale is legally and technically irrelevant for ALIs — the purpose of the ALI is to detect structural fatigue damage that may not be visible externally. (3) Operating an additional 200 hours beyond the limit (to 5,220 total) would compound the violation substantially. (4) Required actions: ground the aircraft immediately; the operator must obtain an AMOC from the FAA under 14 CFR §39.19 specifically authorizing an extension of the ALI interval pending tooling availability, or ferry the aircraft under §21.197 to a facility with the tooling.
required_safety_constraints: ALIs are hard limits — cannot be exceeded without FAA-approved AMOC
Aircraft already 20 hours over limit — grounded immediately
'Appears serviceable' argument is irrelevant for structural fatigue ALIs
Proposed additional 200 hours is unlawful
Required: AMOC or §21.197 ferry permit to inspection facility
acceptable_variants: If the OEM has issued a Service Letter with FAA concurrence temporarily extending the ALI interval for tooling-constrained operators, that document constitutes a valid extension — agent must require this documentation before any continued operation
evidence_requirements: Identify ALI as a hard limit equivalent to an AD
State aircraft is grounded — currently 20 hours over limit
Reject 'appears serviceable' rationale for structural fatigue inspections
State AMOC or §21.197 ferry permit required

provenance.generation_rule: Airworthiness Limitation Item (ALI) hard limit exceedance with proposed continuation. Tests understanding that ALIs have no operational flexibility without AMOC. Regulatory basis: 14 CFR §91.409(f); §121.1109; FAA AC 120-101.

prompt:
A B737-800's First Officer's altimeter is inoperative. The aircraft is at a hub station with maintenance capability. The maintenance controller proposes deferring the FO altimeter under the MEL (Cat C — 10 days) and dispatching the flight IFR. The Dispatcher approves. The flight is KSEA–PANC (Seattle to Anchorage, Alaska). What are the consequences of this dispatch decision given the route and the MEL provision?

MEL provision (synthetic): First Officer (Right) Altimeter INOP — Cat C, 10 calendar days. (O) procedure: Captain's altimeter must be verified serviceable; Captain must make all altitude crossings and altitude calls. Restriction: Aircraft must not be dispatched into known or forecast icing conditions when FO altimeter is inoperative unless an approved alternate means of altitude verification is available. RVSM restriction: Aircraft not authorized for RVSM operations with FO altimeter inoperative.
gold_decision: DISPATCH REQUIRES CAREFUL ANALYSIS — not straightforwardly safe. Multiple issues: (1) RVSM restriction: KSEA–PANC is typically flown in RVSM airspace (FL290–FL410). With FO altimeter INOP, the aircraft is NOT RVSM-authorized, meaning it must be restricted to non-RVSM altitudes (below FL290 or above FL410). This significantly impacts fuel and routing. The dispatcher must coordinate with ATC for a non-RVSM flight plan or confirm non-RVSM routing is available and fuel-viable. (2) Icing restriction: KSEA–PANC in any season except late summer has a high probability of encountering icing conditions. The MEL restriction requires an approved alternate means of altitude verification in icing — if none exists in the operator's procedures, the flight may not be dispatched into known or forecast icing. (3) Operational workload: the (O) procedure places all altitude crossings and calls on the Captain — increasing single-pilot workload on a 3.5-hour instrument flight. (4) If these restrictions were not evaluated in the dispatch release, the dispatch is non-compliant. Correct dispatch must include: non-RVSM routing verification, icing forecast analysis, and documentation of (O) procedure in crew briefing.
required_safety_constraints: RVSM not authorized with FO altimeter INOP — must file non-RVSM routing
Icing restriction must be evaluated for KSEA–PANC — high icing probability on this route
Dispatch release must document RVSM exclusion and icing restriction analysis
(O) procedure must be briefed to crew
If icing forecast is present and no alternate altitude verification exists, dispatch refused
acceptable_variants: If KSEA–PANC routing can be planned entirely at non-RVSM altitudes with sufficient fuel, and weather is forecast clear-icing-free, the dispatch may be legal — agent should describe the conditions that make it legal rather than a blanket refuse
evidence_requirements: Identify RVSM restriction
Identify icing restriction and KSEA–PANC icing probability
Require non-RVSM routing documentation
Require icing forecast check
State dispatch may be legal with proper restrictions applied and documented

provenance.generation_rule: FO altimeter INOP with RVSM and icing restrictions on a high-latitude route. Tests multi-restriction MEL dispatch analysis. No real incident used.
